title: Pfizer — Vaccines — Synthesis
html:
Pfizer Vaccines Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-24 Studies analyzed: 7 Files parsed: Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Final Report v2 (Jan 2026), Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Full Report v1 (Jan 8, 2026), Pneumococcal Listening Session (Dec 2025), Prevnar Adult KO v2, Prevnar Adult Proposal v2, Abrysvo OA HCP-Pt Conversations (embedded in KO appendix), Pneumococcal Full Report (Dec 24/28, Jan 9 versions) Findings extracted: Manual extraction from ~300K chars of parsed PPTX content across all Pfizer vaccine studies Method: Cross-study pattern matching against Vinoth's thesis tracker

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: Communication Style Trumps Clinical Data — Across All Vaccine Types

This is the single most commercially actionable pattern across the entire Pfizer vaccine research portfolio.

Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Final Report (n=20): Assertive/presumptive HCP communication ("I'm going to give you your Prevnar 20 today") produced 100% vaccine acceptance. Suggestive/participatory communication ("Do you want to get the pneumonia vaccine today? No pressure") produced ~60% acceptance. Every single patient who declined or deferred received a suggestive-style recommendation.

Abrysvo OA HCP-Pt Conversations (n=10): 6/10 HCPs took an assertive approach with immediate acceptance; 4/10 suggestive HCPs saw patients defer or decline, with several suggesting the patient "do their own research."

What this means together: The communication style effect is vaccine-agnostic. It persists across pneumococcal and RSV, across healthy and comorbid patients, and across different age groups. This isn't a product insight — it's a portfolio-level behavioral finding. The implication for Pfizer is that investing in HCP communication training (teaching presumptive language) may yield higher vaccination rates than any product-specific messaging campaign.

Pattern 2: Brand-Agnostic Recommendations Are the Norm, Not the Exception

Pneumococcal (n=20): 5/20 HCPs made brand-agnostic recommendations, referring to the "pneumonia shot" or "pneumococcal vaccine" without naming Prevnar 20 or any competitor. These HCPs had the highest rate of dosing misinformation (3/5 incorrectly stated "once every five years").

RSV/Abrysvo (n=10): 8/10 HCPs did not name a specific RSV vaccine brand. Only 2 conversations included a brand-specific recommendation (one Arexvy, one mResvia — neither Abrysvo).

What this means together: Pfizer's products have strong mind-share when HCPs DO mention brands (Prevnar 20 dominates pneumococcal; Abrysvo has dual-indication positioning). But a substantial proportion of HCPs don't mention brands at all. This creates a vulnerability: if the patient goes to a pharmacy, the pharmacist's default — not the physician's brand preference — determines which vaccine the patient receives. For pneumococcal, this means potential Capvaxive substitution; for RSV, potential Arexvy or mResvia substitution.

Pattern 3: Disease Severity Is Systematically Under-Communicated

Pneumococcal (n=20): Disease severity was discussed at a high level in only a minority of conversations. Most HCPs positioned pneumococcal disease as a "manageable risk" rather than life-threatening IPD. Only a handful mentioned sepsis, organ failure, or ICU admission. The report explicitly identified this as an "opportunity to highlight overall burden of disease."

RSV (n=10): 7/10 HCPs described RSV with low severity or no severity discussion at all. Only 3/10 described RSV as potentially severe, mentioning hospitalization risk. The majority compared it to "a run-of-the-mill cold."

What this means together: Across both vaccine categories, HCPs are under-selling the disease threat. This matters because fear-driven acceptance (avoiding hospitalization, preventing invasive disease) was identified as one of the top three motivators for vaccine acceptance. When HCPs don't communicate severity, they rely on "one and done" convenience and CDC authority as the primary drivers — which works for accepting patients but fails for hesitant ones. Pfizer has an opportunity to develop disease severity communication aids that work across the vaccine portfolio.

Pattern 4: In-Office Stock = Same-Day Acceptance; Pharmacy Referral = Drop-Off Risk

Pneumococcal (n=20): 13/17 patients who accepted the vaccine did so because it was available for same-day in-office administration. 4/17 were referred to a pharmacy (2 Prevnar 20, 2 Capvaxive). Pharmacy referral is a recognized "drop-off" risk — patients must take extra steps and may never follow through. One HCP explicitly recommended Capvaxive (PCV21) but didn't have it in stock, creating a Pfizer-favorable default: "Conversely, HCPs often default to Prevnar 20 simply because it is 'in stock today' to avoid losing the patient."

RSV (n=10): 5/10 HCPs stocked RSV vaccines. Those who stocked a vaccine generally recommended it without naming the brand. When a vaccine wasn't stocked, HCPs directed patients to pharmacies or suggested ordering through a pharmacy. One HCP noted difficulty accessing mResvia.

What this means together: The stocking advantage is a major structural moat for Prevnar 20 in pneumococcal — most practices already have it on hand, creating an in-office default. For RSV, stocking is more fragmented, and the pharmacy channel plays a larger role. Pfizer's distribution strategy (ensuring in-office availability) may be as important as its messaging strategy.

Pattern 5: The "One and Done" Message Is Universally Effective but Factually Unstable

Pneumococcal (n=20): "One and done" was the single most frequently cited acceptance driver. Patients responded strongly to the promise of lifetime protection with a single shot. However, 3/5 HCPs who made brand-agnostic recommendations incorrectly stated the vaccine needs repeating every 5 years, and at least one HCP said the vaccine "loses its efficacy over time." This creates a factual inconsistency — some HCPs are selling "one and done" while others are saying "every five years."

RSV (n=10): HCPs positioned the RSV vaccine as "once and done" in 4/10 conversations, with one noting "that might change at some point." No HCPs described it as requiring a booster.

What this means together: "One and done" is the killer messaging, but its factual foundation varies by vaccine and is actively being contradicted by some HCPs. If dosing guidance changes (e.g., boosters become recommended), the entire acceptance framework collapses. Pfizer needs to ensure messaging accuracy while preserving the simplicity that drives acceptance — a delicate balance.

2. Thesis Check

Thesis 1: Capvaxive serotype counting erodes Prevnar 20 share

Status: STRENGTHENED The HCP-Pt conversation data explicitly shows the serotype counting dynamic in action. Multiple HCPs framed Capvaxive as "better" based on higher serotype count and cited specific efficacy percentages (84% vs. 52%). When both vaccines are discussed, Capvaxive wins 3/7 times. Prevnar 20's serotype 4 advantage is never once communicated to patients in any conversation. Action: Propose a competitive tracking study to Pfizer that quantifies serotype counting prevalence and Capvaxive prescription capture rate.

Thesis 2: Abrysvo maternal market won't recover vs. nirsevimab

Status: NO NEW DIRECT EVIDENCE (no maternal-specific study in the current data set) The Abrysvo OA study focused on older adults, not maternal. Q4 2025 earnings show maternal growth in international markets. US maternal trend requires 2025-26 season data. Action: Monitor 2025-26 RSV season coverage data when published.

Thesis 3: Adult RSV market structurally capped

Status: SUPPORTED RSV conversation data shows low disease severity communication (7/10 low/no severity), brand-agnostic recommendations (8/10), and suggestive HCP style (4/10). The combination means the adult RSV market is not generating physician-level urgency. GSK Arexvy US sales declined ~40% in 2025; mResvia sales effectively collapsed ($8M). Action: Frame the research insight as "the RSV adult market needs market development, not just brand promotion" — this positions ZoomRx for a strategic research engagement.

Thesis 4: Presumptive communication = highest ROI intervention

Status: STRONGLY SUPPORTED Confirmed across both pneumococcal and RSV studies with consistent effect sizes. The pattern is vaccine-agnostic and patient-agnostic. Action: This is the insight to lead with in any Pfizer cross-portfolio discussion. Position it as a portfolio-level finding that justifies a behavioral intervention study.

Thesis 5: Penbraya constrained by MenB apathy

Status: NO DIRECT EVIDENCE (no Penbraya-specific study in the current data set) No primary research available. Thesis stands on secondary data (low MenB coverage rates, ACIP shared decision designation). Action: Propose MenB awareness/perceived necessity study to Pfizer Vaccines team.

3. What's Missing (Critical Gaps)

  1. Capvaxive field force tracking: No study currently tracks Merck's field force activity for Capvaxive or its competitive messaging against Prevnar 20. This is the most urgent gap given Thesis 1.

  2. Pharmacy channel dynamics: Both pneumococcal and RSV data show pharmacy referral as a drop-off risk, but no study examines what happens AFTER the referral — does the patient fill the prescription? Does the pharmacist substitute brands? What drives pharmacist vaccine selection?

  3. Abrysvo maternal trend data: No primary research on the maternal vs. nirsevimab dynamic. This is a critical market question for Pfizer but would require a different research design (OB/GYN or pediatrician focus).

  4. Penbraya adoption tracking: No primary data on Penbraya adoption, HCP awareness, or competitive positioning vs. GSK's pentavalent.

  5. Patient follow-through: No study tracks whether patients who defer vaccination actually return for it. The "drop-off" risk is flagged qualitatively but never quantified.

  6. 50-64 age group messaging optimization: The ACIP expansion to 50+ is the biggest market event for pneumococcal, but the conversation data shows this age group is harder to convince — perceived age safety ("I don't reach the exact bracket") is a unique barrier. A focused study on this segment would have high value.

4. Implications for ZoomRx

For Pfizer Client Team (Delivery)

  • Lead all presentations with the communication style finding (assertive vs. suggestive) — it's the most novel and actionable insight
  • Flag the serotype counting competitive risk prominently — Pfizer needs to hear this clearly
  • Recommend a cross-portfolio behavioral intervention study as the next engagement

For BD (Expansion)

  • The pneumococcal HCP-Pt study is complete — the natural follow-up is a Capvaxive competitive tracking study (PET or conversation-based)
  • Pharmacist channel HCP-Pt conversations are a greenfield opportunity that no competitor offers
  • The RSV brand differentiation gap is a $200K+ study waiting to be sold — if Pfizer cares about winning in a 3-player market, they need to understand why their brand isn't being named

For Vinoth (Strategic POV)

  • Pfizer Vaccines is a portfolio play, not a single-product engagement. The cross-portfolio findings (communication style, brand-agnostic recommendations, disease severity under-communication) justify a platform-level research relationship
  • The Capvaxive competitive threat to Prevnar is analogous to Tagrisso's dominance vs. Rybrevant — established brand inertia vs. compelling new entrant data. The playbook Pfizer needs mirrors what J&J needs in lung. This cross-client pattern is worth articulating

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
